name: Alvaro Luna Hernandez
description: Chicano political prisoner, prolific jailhouse lawyer, and longtime member of the Movimiento de Liberacion Nacional Mexicano. Serving a life sentence for capital murder out of Pecos County, Texas. According to documents obtained after trial, the state used perjured testimony and suppressed crime-lab reports and other favorable evidence that would have cleared him; he has consistently maintained his innocence. While incarcerated he became a leading prison-reform litigator in Texas, contributing to the landmark Ruiz v. Estelle systemwide class action and to a civil-rights suit charging the Texas Governor and Parole Board with racial discrimination against Chicano and Black prisoners. Spent eight consecutive years in solitary confinement in retaliation for his organizing — a 1984 prison report justified the isolation by quoting his "own statements concerning political beliefs." Held at Goree Unit and elsewhere in the Texas Department of Criminal Justice. Eligible for parole since 1986 but repeatedly denied.

cases.convicted: Yes — convicted by all-white jury; state later shown to have used perjured testimony and suppressed favorable evidence

name: Alberto Aranda
description: Chicano political prisoner active with the Movimiento de Liberacion Nacional Mexicano. Imprisoned in the Texas Department of Criminal Justice since 1980 on an 18-year sentence for a robbery of approximately $1.50. Politicized during the 1980 Ruiz v. Estelle prisoners' civil-rights trial, Aranda devoted himself to legal and organizing work against the Texas system's "slave plantation" conditions. Spent nearly nine years in solitary confinement as punishment for activism, including more than two years confined twenty-two and a half hours per day in a specially constructed "management cell" on death row despite having no death sentence. Convicted in 1989 in a Huntsville district court of aggravated assault on a prison guard and sentenced to an additional 40 years. Held at the Ellis I Unit.

cases.charges: Robbery (approximately $1.50); aggravated assault on a prison guard (Huntsville, 1989)
cases.convicted: Yes — original 18-year robbery sentence + 40 years for 1989 prison-guard assault

name: Rita D. Brown
description: Working-class lesbian revolutionary and former member of the George Jackson Brigade, a multi-racial armed organization that operated in the Pacific Northwest in the mid-to-late 1970s in solidarity with Native sovereignty struggles, the Seattle auto workers' strike, and Washington State prisoners' human-rights organizing. Brown spent eight and a half years in federal women's prisons, including nearly a year in isolation in Davis Hall at Alderson — the first special control unit for political women in the federal system — where she was held alongside Assata Shakur and reactionary and Nazi prisoners as a deliberate threat. Subjected to extra-long isolation and harassment because of her lesbian identity. After release became a leading voice on white anti-imperialist political prisoners; delivered the speech 'The White North American Political Prisoners in the U.S.' at the International Tribunal on October 3, 1992.

cases.institution_name: Federal Bureau of Prisons (incl. Federal Reformatory for Women, Alderson)
cases.charges: Federal charges related to George Jackson Brigade armed actions in the Pacific Northwest

name: Mark Cook
description: Black member of the George Jackson Brigade, a multi-racial armed organization that operated in the Pacific Northwest in the mid-to-late 1970s in solidarity with Native sovereignty, the Seattle auto workers' strike, and Washington State prisoners' rights organizing. Convicted of participation in the 1976 jailbreak of fellow Brigade member John Sherman from a Seattle hospital and related armed actions. Held in federal and state custody for decades; named in 1992 by Rita 'Bo' Brown as one of the two remaining George Jackson Brigade prisoners. Released after roughly twenty years.

cases.charges: Aiding the 1976 escape of George Jackson Brigade comrade John Sherman; related armed actions
cases.sentence: Multiple consecutive life terms (later paroled after roughly 20 years)

name: Ed Mead
description: White working-class ex-prisoner and member of the George Jackson Brigade, a multi-racial armed organization active in the Pacific Northwest in the mid-to-late 1970s. Captured in 1976 in Tukwila, Washington during an attempted bank expropriation. While incarcerated at Washington State Penitentiary at Walla Walla he founded Men Against Sexism, organizing imprisoned men against prisoner-on-prisoner rape — work the prison administration repeatedly retaliated against. Cited in 1992 by Rita 'Bo' Brown as one of the two remaining George Jackson Brigade prisoners. Released after roughly eighteen years.

cases.charges: Attempted bank robbery (Tukwila, Washington, 1976); George Jackson Brigade armed actions

name: Larry Giddings
description: Anti-authoritarian prisoner accused alongside co-defendant Bill Dunne of being a member of a small, heavily armed revolutionary group associated with the Wellspring Communion. Convicted in 1979 for the attempted liberation of comrades from Seattle's King County Jail; the operation was alleged to have been financed by bank expropriations and facilitated by the illegal acquisition of weapons and explosives. Paroled from federal prison in 2004 after approximately 25 years.

cases.charges: Aiding and abetting escape; bank robbery; weapons offenses (Seattle King County Jail liberation attempt, 1979)

name: Richard Picariello
description: White anti-authoritarian prisoner convicted in 1977 of armed actions against U.S. oppression and imperialism. Originally due for release after fifteen years, his sentence was repeatedly extended by federal authorities because of his political organizing inside prison. Held in federal custody for decades.

cases.charges: Armed actions against U.S. government / corporate targets

name: Peter Lumsdaine
description: Anti-nuclear Plowshares activist who, with Keith Kjoller, used axes and sledgehammers on November 14, 1991 to destroy a Navstar GPS satellite at the Rockwell International plant in Seal Beach, California — at the time a key component of the U.S. first-strike capability, valued at approximately $2.5 million. Convicted in federal court of destruction of government property and related charges. Sentenced to 18 months in federal prison.

cases.charges: Destruction of government property; conspiracy (Navstar GPS satellite Plowshares action, Seal Beach 1991)

name: Keith Kjoller
description: Anti-nuclear Plowshares activist who, with Peter Lumsdaine, used axes and sledgehammers on November 14, 1991 to destroy a Navstar GPS satellite at the Rockwell International plant in Seal Beach, California — at the time a key component of the U.S. first-strike capability, valued at approximately $2.5 million. Convicted in federal court of destruction of government property and related charges. Sentenced to 18 months in federal prison.

cases.charges: Destruction of government property; conspiracy (Navstar GPS satellite Plowshares action, Seal Beach 1991)

name: Gillam Kerley
description: Anti-draft and anti-war organizer who refused to register for the Selective Service in the early 1980s as the Reagan administration revived peacetime registration. One of fewer than 20 men ever indicted under the Solomon amendment-era prosecutions, he was sentenced to three years in federal prison and a $10,000 fine — not merely for failing to register but, as the prosecution conceded, for persisting in publicly organizing other young men against registration and the draft. The lead organizer of the National Resistance Committee, his case became a focal point of 1980s anti-draft campaigns.

cases.charges: Failure to register for Selective Service; organizing against draft registration

name: Silvia Baraldini
description: Italian-born North American anti-imperialist sentenced in 1983 to 43 years in federal prison — 40 years on the underlying charges plus 3 for criminal contempt — for aiding the November 2, 1979 escape of Black Liberation Army member Assata Shakur from the Clinton Correctional Facility for Women in New Jersey, and for RICO conspiracy involving the May 19th Communist Organization. Imprisoned in 1986 at the Lexington High Security Unit alongside Susan Rosenberg and Puerto Rican POW Alejandrina Torres; the unit was closed in 1988 after a successful national and international campaign. The Italian government repeatedly requested her repatriation to serve her sentence in Italy under the Strasbourg Convention; the U.S. Department of Justice refused for years, citing concerns Italian sentences would be 'too lenient.' Diagnosed in prison with an aggressive uterine cancer that was repeatedly ignored. Finally repatriated to Italy on August 24, 1999.

cases.charges: RICO conspiracy; aiding the 1979 escape of Assata Shakur; criminal contempt

name: Raymond Luc Levasseur
description: Vietnam veteran, former Massachusetts prisoner, and lead defendant of the 'Ohio 7' — members of the Sam Melville-Jonathan Jackson Unit and United Freedom Front (UFF), armed clandestine organizations that carried out bombings of U.S. government, military, and corporate offices between the mid-1970s and mid-1980s in opposition to U.S. support for South African apartheid and U.S. intervention in Central America. Captured November 4, 1984 after the largest manhunt in New Jersey police history. Convicted of bombings of unoccupied military targets and sentenced to 45 years in federal prison; acquitted by a Massachusetts jury in 1989 of sedition charges in a separate prosecution. Sent directly to the Marion Control Unit after sentencing. Released on parole in 2004 after twenty years.

cases.charges: Bombings of U.S. military and corporate targets; RICO conspiracy

name: Richard C. Williams
description: Member of the United Freedom Front and one of the 'Ohio 7' — clandestine anti-imperialist combatants who bombed U.S. military and corporate targets in opposition to South African apartheid and U.S. intervention in Central America. Captured November 4, 1984. Convicted with co-defendant Thomas Manning for the December 21, 1981 killing of New Jersey State Police trooper Philip Lamonaco during a routine traffic stop on Interstate 80; also convicted of multiple bombings. Held in federal control units and ultimately at FMC Butner. Died in prison on December 6, 2005.

cases.charges: Murder of NJ State Trooper Philip Lamonaco (Dec 21 1981); bombings of military / corporate targets; RICO conspiracy
cases.sentence: 45 years federal + 23 years New Jersey (died in prison 2005)

name: Patricia Gros Levasseur
description: Member of the United Freedom Front and 'Ohio 7' household; partner of Raymond Luc Levasseur. Captured November 4, 1984 in Deerfield, Ohio after the largest manhunt in New Jersey police history. Convicted on harboring and conspiracy charges related to the UFF's clandestine bombings of U.S. military and corporate targets. Released on parole in 1989.

name: Judith A. Clark
description: White anti-imperialist sentenced to 75 years to life for her role as a getaway driver in the October 20, 1981 attempted expropriation of a Brink's armored truck at the Nanuet Mall in Rockland County, New York. The action, claimed by the Revolutionary Armed Task Force — a coalition of Black Liberation Army veterans and white anti-imperialists from the May 19th Communist Organization — left two Nyack police officers and a Brink's guard dead. Held for over thirty years at Bedford Hills Correctional Facility, where she helped found the AIDS Counseling and Education program (ACE) that became a national model for peer HIV education in U.S. women's prisons. Sentence commuted by Governor Andrew Cuomo in December 2016; granted parole April 2019.

cases.charges: Felony murder x3 (Brink's expropriation, Nyack NY, October 20 1981)
